questions.question_text: What is a key feature of arrow functions in JavaScript?

choices.text: They use a more modern, shorter syntax for writing functions
They require a return keyword in all cases
They are used only inside classes

questions.question_text: What does implicit return mean in an arrow function?

choices.text: The function automatically returns the result of the expression without using the return keyword
The function will always return undefined
The function will throw an error if no return keyword is used

questions.question_text: Which of the following uses implicit return correctly?

choices.text: const add = (x, y) => { return x + y; }
const add = x, y => x + y;

questions.question_text: If you want to add more lines of code before your return statement in an arrow function, what should you do?

choices.text: Use curly braces and add a return statement
Add a comma after each line
Use parentheses instead of curly braces
Wrap the code in an array

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT true about arrow functions?

choices.text: They cannot be used to define anonymous functions (they must have a name)
They are a more modern alternative to traditional function expressions
They need to be called to run
They can be used for both one-liners and multi-line functions

questions.question_text: What is a valid way to write an arrow function that takes one parameter and returns a greeting?
Which of the following arrow functions will NOT return the square of a number correctly?
What happens if you use curly braces in an arrow function but forget to write return?

choices.text: The function will throw an error
The function will return the last expression

questions.question_text: Which of these arrow functions is incorrectly written?

choices.text: const sum = (a, b) => { return a + b; }

questions.question_text: What is the main reason developers use loops in code?

choices.text: To repeat a task multiple times automatically
To confuse people reading the code
To run a block of code one time only
To stop the program from running

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes a for loop?

choices.text: A structure that repeats code a specific number of times
A block of code that runs forever
A way to repeat code while a condition is true
A function that runs code when a button is clicked

questions.question_text: What does i++ do in a for loop?

choices.text: Increases the value of i by 1

questions.question_text:
Look at this code:

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  console.log("hi");
}

How many times will “hi” be logged?

In this loop, what is the starting value of i?

for (let i = 5; i < 10; i++) {
  console.log(i);
}
In a typical for loop, which of the following is NOT one of the three required parts in the parentheses?

What will this code print?

const nums = [10, 20, 30];
for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
  console.log(nums[i]);
}
What happens if a loop never reaches its stopping condition?
Which of the following would correctly loop through an array of 5 items?

What is the value of i after this loop ends?

for (let i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
  console.log(i);
}
What is the purpose of an if statement in JavaScript?

choices.text: To run code only when a condition is true

questions.question_text: What will this code output?
What is the purpose of the else block?

choices.text: To run code if the if condition is false
To run code before the if block

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the correct syntax for else if?

choices.text: else if (x > 10) { //code to run if true}
if else (x > 10) { //code to run if true}
else if { //code to run if true}

questions.question_text: What will this code output?
What happens if none of the if or else if conditions are true, and there is no else block?

choices.text: Nothing happens, the code between the curly braces will not run and the browser will move on to the next line of code.

questions.question_text: Why would a developer use else if instead of multiple if statements?

choices.text: To group conditions that depend on each other

questions.question_text: What is the correct way to define a function in JavaScript?
Why do we use functions in JavaScript?

choices.text: So that code runs only when we want it to

questions.question_text: What happens when you define a function but don’t call it?

choices.text: Nothing happens until you call it

questions.question_text:
What does this function do?

function greet() {
   console.log("Hi!");
}

choices.text: It stores code that will run later when we call greet()
It prints “Hi!” right away when the page loads
It adds HTML to the page

questions.question_text: Which line correctly calls a function named startGame?
Which operator is used for logical AND in JavaScript?
Which operator represents logical OR?
What does the ! (NOT) operator do?
What does this code output?
What will this code log?
What will this log?
What will this code print?
What condition would allow this to run console.log("Discount applied")?

choices.text: If at least one is true

questions.question_text: What will this code output?
What will this code output?
Which condition would let this message print: "Send email"?

choices.text: If user is not a guest and their email is verified

questions.question_text: What does this check?

choices.text: One or both must be true

questions.question_text:
Refer to the addPoints() function.

At the addPoints() function declaration, what is the value of 'points'?

choices.text: It does not have value yet

questions.question_text: The addPoints() function uses a parameter called points. Where in the program does 'points' get its value?

choices.text: In the single function calls, addPoints is called and 50 is passed in as the argument
The earnBonus() function calls the addPoints function and passes in bonusPoints as the argument.
The awardAchievement() function calls addPoints() and passes in pointReward as an argument

questions.question_text: In the addPoints() function, why do we want points to be dynamic?

choices.text: Because every player will have a different number of points to add to, and we want to be able to use the same function for all players, instead of making a different function for every possible points value.
We don't know what the value of points is yet
We don't want to use global scope for points

questions.question_text: When updatePlayer() is declared, what are the values of name and level?

choices.text: Parameters do not have values when the function is declared, so we don't know yet

questions.question_text: How many pieces of data does the updatePlayer() function need to run?
What happens to the global variables when updatePlayer() function runs?

choices.text: They get reassigned to be the value of name and level
"Jordan" and 1 get passed into the function
They are not used in this function

questions.question_text: What does earnBonus() do with the bonusPoints variable?

choices.text: Passes the variable into the addPoints() function as an argument

questions.question_text:
When earnBonus calls addPoints(bonusPoints), what value gets passed to the 'points' parameter?

NOTE: take your time on this one! You will have to follow the data to find the answer.
Why is it useful for one function to call another?

choices.text: We can pass local variables to the next function and avoid using too many globals

Large, complicated tasks become easier to understand and manage when broken into smaller pieces. For example, earnBonus() has one job (calculate bonus points) and then hands off the separate job of updating the score to addPoints(). Each function has a clear, single responsibility.

If you need to change how something works, you only change it in one place. If the way points are added needs to change (maybe add sound effects or animations), you only update the addPoints() function, and all the other functions that call it automatically get the improvement.

questions.question_text:
The function processGameResults() 

  1. Calculates the winRate and the winPercentage.
  2. Logs a sentence for testing to the console. 
  3. Calls displayStats and passes in winRate, along with two other parameters
When displayStats() is called, what value does 'percentage' receive?
Why are the parameter names in displayStats() different from the argument names?

choices.text: The parameters when the function is declared are placeholders. We can pass in any value as an argument when the function is called. Either a hard coded value or a different variable from our program
They should not be different, that is not best practices
You can always change the parameter name to match the argument

questions.question_text: Trace the data: wins becomes what parameter in displayStats?

awardAchievement is called 3 times with different arguments. 
What will achievementName be in the third call?

awardAchievement is called 3 times with different arguments. 
What will pointReward be in the second call?
If you did the bonus, add your link here. If you skipped the bonus this time, type N/A here.
What is the weekly schedule for advanced classes?
What happens if an advanced student cannot complete an assignment by the due date?

choices.text: They must submit the late statement
They must email the instructor explaining why
They will be automatically dropped from the course

questions.question_text: When are assignments due for advanced students?

choices.text: It depends, please refer to your assignment due dates
End of Block C on Thursday
Monday morning of the following week

questions.question_text: What must happen before a student can move on to the next course?

choices.text: All assignments for the current course must be completed

questions.question_text: What should advanced students do when copying code from the internet?

choices.text: Cite the source URL and add a comment explaining what the code does
Rewrite it in their own words
Paste it as-is with no comment

questions.question_text: What happens if you turn in another student’s code as your own?

choices.text: The assignment will be marked as incomplete
You must credit the student in Slack
It's ok as long as you include comments

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of a Professional Improvement Plan (PIP)?

choices.text: To provide structured support and help students reach their professional goals
To punish students for being behind
To require students to reapply for the program

questions.question_text: In order to be clocked in, you...

choices.text: Can login from a computer, are able to be upright, camera on
Can login from a phone so you can hear
Can run errands as long as you have your hot spot
Can work on other assignments during lecture

questions.question_text: Although we highly encourage people to continue to learn outside of class time, when are you allowed to fulfill your 25 hours of paid work?

choices.text: Only during class hours. We do not offer flexible hours.
Whenever I want, as long as I work for 25 hours
If I'm 20 mins late, I can make that up later and still clock in for the full five hours
If I have an important doctor's appointment on Friday, I can make it up on the weekend.
